title: Measurement
objective: Students will explore measurement concepts including Area (age 8+), Understanding Area, Area by Tiling and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Area (age 8+). Get ready for an adventure!
Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft)
Time to try this out: Count unit squares to find the area of an L-shaped figure
If you draws a shape on squared centimetre paper, can they find its area by counting the squares — including making a sensible estimate for any partial squares?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Area (age 8+). Count unit squares to find the area of an L-shaped figure Measure the area of a book cover using square-centimetre tiles
Have you ever thought about Understanding Area? Let's find out together!
Understand that a unit square has one square unit of area and that the area of a plane figure is the number of unit squares that cover it without gaps or overlaps
Time to try this out: Identify a unit square and state its area is 1 square unit
If you counts the small squares inside a rectangle drawn on squared paper, can they tell you the area — and explain what "one square unit" means?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Understanding Area. Identify a unit square and state its area is 1 square unit Explain why a figure covered by 12 unit squares has area 12 square units
Today we're going to learn about Area by Tiling. Get ready for an adventure!
Find the area of a rectangle by tiling it with unit squares and show that the result equals the product of the side lengths
Let's practice together! Tile a 4×6 rectangle and count 24 squares, then verify 4×6=24
If a rectangular room is 4 m long and 3 m wide, can you work out its area by tiling it with 1-metre squares in their head — and explain why that equals 4 × 3?
Great job! Today we learned about Area by Tiling. Tile a 4×6 rectangle and count 24 squares, then verify 4×6=24 Explain why the number of rows times the number in each row gives the area
Let's discover the amazing world of Understanding angles (age 8+)!
Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas
Time to try this out: Calculate the area of a 7 cm × 9 cm rectangle as 63 cm²
If you needs to carpet a room that is 5 m by 4 m, can they work out how much carpet to buy — using multiplication rather than counting squares?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Understanding angles (age 8+): Calculate the area of a 7 cm × 9 cm rectangle as 63 cm² Draw a rectangle with area 36 square units and label its side lengths
Today we're going to learn about Area and the distributive property. Get ready for an adventure!
Use tiling to demonstrate the distributive property: the area of a rectangle with sides a and (b+c) equals a×b + a×c; use area models to represent the distributive property
Time to try this out: Tile a 3×(4+2) rectangle and show it decomposes into 3×4 and 3×2
If you wants to work out 6 × 13 by splitting it into 6 × 10 and 6 × 3, can they draw a rectangle divided into two parts to show why that method works?
Great job! Today we learned about Area and the distributive property. Tile a 3×(4+2) rectangle and show it decomposes into 3×4 and 3×2 Use an area model to compute 6×13 as 6×10 + 6×3
Let's discover the amazing world of Converting measurement units!
Convert between different units of measure (e.g. kilometre to metre, hour to minute, minute to second, year to month, week to day)
Time to try this out: Convert 3 km to 3000 m
If you is told a journey is 3.5 km, can they convert that to metres — and also turn 2 hours 15 minutes into just minutes?
Great job! Today we learned about Converting measurement units. Convert 3 km to 3000 m State 2 hours = 120 minutes
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Perimeters of polygons!
Solve problems involving perimeters of polygons: find perimeter from side lengths, find an unknown side length, and explore rectangles with same perimeter but different areas (or vice versa)
Time to try this out: Calculate the perimeter of a rectangle with sides 8 cm and 5 cm
If a rectangle has a perimeter of 24 cm and one side is 8 cm, can you work out the length of the other side — without drawing it?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Perimeters of polygons: Calculate the perimeter of a rectangle with sides 8 cm and 5 cm Find the missing side of a pentagon with perimeter 30 cm and four known sides
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Numbers on a number line!
Solve word problems involving elapsed time by adding and subtracting time intervals in minutes, including using a number line
Let me show you something cool. A film starts at 2:15 and lasts 47 minutes — when does it end?
If you starts football practice at 3:20 p.m. and it lasts 75 minutes, can they work out what time it finishes — using a number line to help?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Numbers on a number line. A film starts at 2:15 and lasts 47 minutes — when does it end? Calculate how many minutes between 9:20 and 10:05
Let's discover the amazing world of Halves and quarters (age 8+)!
Generate measurement data by measuring lengths to the nearest half and quarter inch; display the data on a line plot with a scale marked in whole numbers, halves, and quarters
Let me show you something cool. Measure five objects to the nearest 1/4 inch
If you measures ten pencil lengths to the nearest quarter inch, can they record the data and plot it on a number line split into quarters — then spot which length appears most?
Great job! Today we learned about Halves and quarters (age 8+). Measure five objects to the nearest 1/4 inch Create a line plot showing the lengths of classmates' pencils in half-inches
Let's discover the amazing world of Telling time to the minute (age 8+)!
Tell and write time to the nearest minute using analogue and digital clocks
Let me show you something cool. Read 7:43 from an analogue clock face
If a clock's minute hand points just past the 9, can you work out the exact time to the nearest minute — and write it in digital format?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Telling time to the minute (age 8+): Read 7:43 from an analogue clock face Write 11:06 on a digital display given a clock with hands
Today we're going to learn about Estimating and comparing money. Get ready for an adventure!
Estimate, compare, and calculate different measures including money in pounds and pence
Let me show you something cool. Estimate the length of the classroom in metres
If you is comparing three items costing £2.85, £3.10, and £2.50, can they put them in order from cheapest to most expensive — and work out the total cost?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Estimating and comparing money. Estimate the length of the classroom in metres Compare 1.5 kg and 1200 g and identify which is heavier
Have you ever thought about Measuring Liquids & Masses? Let's find out together!
Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using grams, kilograms, and litres; solve one-step word problems involving mass or volume
Let me show you something cool. Estimate the mass of a textbook in grams or kilograms
If you is filling bottles that each hold 250 ml from a 2-litre container of juice, can they work out how many bottles they can fill?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Measuring Liquids & Masses. Estimate the mass of a textbook in grams or kilograms Read a scale to measure liquid volume in litres
Today we're going to learn about Area of compound shapes. Get ready for an adventure!
Recognise area as additive; find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing into non-overlapping rectangles and summing their areas
Let me show you something cool. Decompose an L-shape into two rectangles, find each area, and add them
If you is given an L-shaped room on squared paper, can they split it into two rectangles, find the area of each part, and add them to get the total area?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Area of compound shapes. Decompose an L-shape into two rectangles, find each area, and add them Find the area of a floor plan shaped like a T by splitting into rectangles
Today we're going to learn about 12-hour and 24-hour time. Get ready for an adventure!
Read, write, and convert time between analogue and digital 12-hour and 24-hour clocks
Let's practice together! Convert 3:45 pm to 15:45 in 24-hour time
If a train timetable shows a departure at 14:35, can you convert that to 12-hour clock time — and tell you whether that's morning or afternoon?
You did it! Here's what we explored about 12-hour and 24-hour time: Convert 3:45 pm to 15:45 in 24-hour time Read 19:30 and state the 12-hour equivalent as 7:30 pm
